steps.title: Recognize that both equations describe the same line
steps.content:
Multiply the first equation by 5:

$$5(4x+9y)=5(11)\Rightarrow 20x+45y=55$$

This is exactly the second equation. So the system is really one line: $4x+9y=11$. Any point on that line satisfies both equations.

steps.title: Check each choice against $4x+9y=11$
steps.content: A point $(x,y)$ works for every real $r$ only if substituting it into $4x+9y=11$ always gives $11$. For Choice A — $(r,-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9})$, $x=r$ and $y=-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9}$, so $$4r+9\left(-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9}\right)=4r-4r+11=11.$$ Choice A — $(r,-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9})$ works for all $r$.

steps.title: Eliminate the others
steps.content: Choice B — $(-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9},r)$ gives $\frac{65r+44}{9}$, which is not always $11$. Choices C and D include constants $11$ and $55$, making $4x+9y$ far larger than $11$. Therefore Choice A — $(r,-\frac{4r}{9}+\frac{11}{9})$.
